1.description: Comparison of monthly payments with IDR and standard repayment plans The sample consists of student loans that receive forbearance out of loan servicers’ discrete decisions with a cumulative duration of 12 months or more, and are followed by continued forbearance, income-driven repayment requiring zero monthly payment (IDR), or deferment. Panel A presents hypothetical monthly student loan payment under IDR plans requiring 10% of discretionary income and the standard 10-year repayment plans for student loans that continue with forbearance after a minimum of prior 12-month duration. The dashed line represents the average monthly payment under the standard 10-year plans (corresponding to the right y-axis); the solid line represents the average payment under IDR plans for different family sizes (corresponding to the left x-axis). Panel B presents hypothetical monthly income that would warrant a higher monthly repayment under IDR plans (requiring 10% of students’ discretionary income) than that under the standard 10-year repayment plans for student loans that continue with forbearance after a minimum of prior 12-month duration. The dashed line represents the average observed annual income of loan borrowers who continue with forbearance after a minimum of prior 12-month duration (corresponding to the right y-axis); the solid line represents the average hypothetical income making IDR repayment to exceed the standard payment for different family sizes (corresponding to the left x-axis). Detailed estimation method and parameters, including initial borrowed amount, interest rate, student income, and poverty lines, are described in Section 2.2. The bars surrounding each data point represent the 10% and 90% confidence intervals. SOURCE: U.S. Department of Education, Institute of Education Sciences, National Center for Education Statistics, 2012/17 Beginning Postsecondary Students Longitudinal Study (BPS:12/17). NOTE: Estimates are weighted based on survey weights for panel analysis.

2.description: Evolution of student loan balances and subsequent loan delinquency The sample consists of student loans that receive forbearance out of loan servicers’ discrete decisions with a cumulative duration of 12 months or more, and are followed by continued forbearance, income-driven repayment requiring zero monthly payment (IDR), or deferment. Only federal subsidized student loans are included. Switch equals one if the loan’s repayment plan is switched to either IDR or deferment and equals zero if the repayment plan continues in forbearance. In panel A, the three columns present (1) the initial student loan balance (normalized to $100), (2) the outstanding balance after 12-month minimum prior forbearance, and (3) the delinquency rates as of December 2017. Delinquency is identified if a student fails to make loan payments for up to 270 days. The left y-axis corresponds to columns 1 and 2, and the right y-axis corresponds to column 3. Panel B plots outstanding student loan balance over time following repayment options, relative to the beginning balance at the time when the repayment options begin. The y-axis presents outstanding balance as a ratio of the beginning balance at the time when the repayment options begin. The x-axis denotes the time since the beginning of repayment options. The shaded bands surrounding each line represent the 10% and 90% confidence intervals. SOURCE: U.S. Department of Education, Institute of Education Sciences, National Center for Education Statistics, 2012/17 Beginning Postsecondary Students Longitudinal Study (BPS:12/17). NOTE: Estimates are weighted based on survey weights for panel analysis.

3.description: Proportion of loans receiving different repayment options, by borrower gender and race The sample consists of student loans that receive forbearance out of loan servicers’ discrete decisions with a cumulative duration of 12 months or more, and are followed by continued forbearance, income-driven repayment requiring zero monthly payment (IDR), or deferment. Only federal subsidized student loans are included. The x-axis denotes the proportion. The bars surrounding each column represent the 10% and 90% confidence intervals. SOURCE: U.S. Department of Education, Institute of Education Sciences, National Center for Education Statistics, 2012/17 Beginning Postsecondary Students Longitudinal Study (BPS:12/17). NOTE: Estimates are weighted based on survey weights for panel analysis.

4.description: Effects of gender and race on loan management This figure decomposes the effect of gender and race on loan management. The left (lighter) section plots the coefficients of Female, conditioning on White=1 and White=0 respectively. The right (darker) section plots the coefficients of White, conditioning on Female=1 and Female=0 respectively. The hollow and solid points represent specifications with different controls, corresponding to those in columns (4) and (5) of Table 1 Panel B. Bars surrounding each point represent the 10% and 90% confidence interval. SOURCE: U.S. Department of Education, Institute of Education Sciences, National Center for Education Statistics, 2012/17 Beginning Postsecondary Students Longitudinal Study (BPS:12/17). NOTE: Estimates are weighted based on survey weights for panel analysis.

5.description: Loan servicer compensation Panel A presents the contracted compensation that a loan servicer receives from the DOE as of 2014, by loan status. Contracted compensation is obtained from the DOE’s website (https://studentaid.gov/data-center/business-info/contracts/loan-servicing) and refers to the price per loan serviced by the servicer. Panels B and C delineate servicer revenues under various borrower repayment scenarios. Each node of the charts denotes a possible loan status, and each number in the parentheses of a node presents compensation per loan. Panel B pertains to the case in which a loan’s repayment plan is switched to IDR after the loan has received prior forbearance for at least 12 months. Panel C pertains to the case in which a loan’s repayment continues with forbearance following 12-month prior forbearance. A detailed description of each path can be found in Section 4.3.
